reviews.summary: This work extrapolates the concept of Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) modulo model invariances to differentiable tree ensembles (DTE). The authors revealed that, in contrast to neural networks (NNs), permutation invariance is insufficient to provide LMC in DTE and propose two additional tree-specific invariances that enable LMC after taking them into account: subtree flip invariance and splitting order invariance. In addition, they provide a modified DTE architecture that does not posses these additional invariances, however still enjoys LMC with only permutation invariance akin to neural network models. This work proposes two algorithms for building LMC given two independently trained DTEs, based on similar methods from NN LMC literature. The claims are supported by a detailed empirical evaluation.
reviews.strengths:
Honestly, I enjoyed reading this paper. Although I am not specialized in tree ensembles, I have certain expertise in LMC, and was pleased to find that it is also relevant for DTE models. I think that this contribution is novel and significant.

The paper is very well-structured. It was very easy to follow despite having no significant experience in decision trees, the authors did a good job preparing the reader in Sec. 2. 

Section 3 presents the main contributions of this work, which is done very well using both detailed and intuitive text description and auxiliary images illustrating the main concepts.

Empirical evaluation is excellent, involving multiple datasets, hyperparameter options, and random seeds. The authors tackled many important questions concerning the study of LMC in DTEs and even compared with NN LMC, which I specifically liked.
reviews.weaknesses:
It is hard for me to formulate substantial flaws in this work but a couple of remarks that I put in the next section. 

The main weakness of this work is lack of theoretical support and practical implications. However, I acknowledge that these are the same limitations that are attributed to LMC in neural networks, which is a significantly more broad and well-studied field than LMC in tree ensembles. I hope that future work will address these disadvantages in some way. 

Also, I believe that the text could be slightly polished to eliminate typos and small inaccuracies. For instance, the value $D$ in line 127 is not defined at its first occurrence.
reviews.questions:
Below I list some questions/comments related to the work.
1. It is indeed surprising that mode connectivity can worsen compared to a naive interpolation after accounting for the permutation invariance (e.g., Fig. 1). To my view, the barriers of naive interpolation (realizing an identity permutation) must upper bound the barriers after permutation search. I would ask the authors to give a small comment on this.
2. Is the weighting strategy described in Sec. 3.2 remains the same for oblivious DTEs that share the same parameters for all nodes at equal depth, so every parameter affects all leaves?
3. Interestingly, according to Figure 5, non-oblivious DTE shows better LMC than oblivious DTE when accounting for the same invariances. I suppose that this benign over-parameterization effect is similar to increasing $M$ in DTE or width in NN. What do the authors think?
4. Figure 7: I would suggest adding the result of common (combined data) training for comparison alike [1].
5. Does LMC in DTE suffer from the same variance collapse issues reported by [2]? If do, how could it be repaired and could it additionally improve LMC in DTE?
6. It is a little astonishing how much worse Activation Matching performs in DTE compared to Weight Matching. In NN LMC (based on literature and my personal experience), this difference is not so prominent. Could the authors comment on this a little more?
7. At the same depth, (modified) decision lists contain less total parameters than oblivious trees and especially non-oblivious trees. However, all DTE architectures perform very similarly according to Tab. 3 and 4. Could the authors give any explanation of this effect?
8. Would adding subtree flip invariance in the terminal splitting node of regular decision lists improve their LMC reported in Tab. 3 and 4? 

[1] Samuel Ainsworth, Jonathan Hayase, and Siddhartha Srinivasa. Git Re-Basin: Merging Models modulo Permutation Symmetries. In The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations, 2023. 

[2] Keller Jordan, Hanie Sedghi, Olga Saukh, Rahim Entezari, and Behnam Neyshabur. REPAIR: REnormalizing permuted activations for interpolation repair. In The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations, 2023.
reviews.limitations: The authors discuss the limitations of their methods in Section 3.2.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This work extrapolates the concept of Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) modulo model invariances to differentiable tree ensembles (DTE). The authors revealed that, in contrast to neural networks (NNs), permutation invariance is insufficient to provide LMC in DTE and propose two additional tree-specific invariances that enable LMC after taking them into account: subtree flip invariance and splitting order invariance. In addition, they provide a modified DTE architecture that does not posses these additional invariances, however still enjoys LMC with only permutation invariance akin to neural network models. This work proposes two algorithms for building LMC given two independently trained DTEs, based on similar methods from NN LMC literature. The claims are supported by a detailed empirical evaluation.

Strengths:
Honestly, I enjoyed reading this paper. Although I am not specialized in tree ensembles, I have certain expertise in LMC, and was pleased to find that it is also relevant for DTE models. I think that this contribution is novel and significant.

The paper is very well-structured. It was very easy to follow despite having no significant experience in decision trees, the authors did a good job preparing the reader in Sec. 2. 

Section 3 presents the main contributions of this work, which is done very well using both detailed and intuitive text description and auxiliary images illustrating the main concepts.

Empirical evaluation is excellent, involving multiple datasets, hyperparameter options, and random seeds. The authors tackled many important questions concerning the study of LMC in DTEs and even compared with NN LMC, which I specifically liked.

Weaknesses:
It is hard for me to formulate substantial flaws in this work but a couple of remarks that I put in the next section. 

The main weakness of this work is lack of theoretical support and practical implications. However, I acknowledge that these are the same limitations that are attributed to LMC in neural networks, which is a significantly more broad and well-studied field than LMC in tree ensembles. I hope that future work will address these disadvantages in some way. 

Also, I believe that the text could be slightly polished to eliminate typos and small inaccuracies. For instance, the value $D$ in line 127 is not defined at its first occurrence.

Questions:
Below I list some questions/comments related to the work.
1. It is indeed surprising that mode connectivity can worsen compared to a naive interpolation after accounting for the permutation invariance (e.g., Fig. 1). To my view, the barriers of naive interpolation (realizing an identity permutation) must upper bound the barriers after permutation search. I would ask the authors to give a small comment on this.
2. Is the weighting strategy described in Sec. 3.2 remains the same for oblivious DTEs that share the same parameters for all nodes at equal depth, so every parameter affects all leaves?
3. Interestingly, according to Figure 5, non-oblivious DTE shows better LMC than oblivious DTE when accounting for the same invariances. I suppose that this benign over-parameterization effect is similar to increasing $M$ in DTE or width in NN. What do the authors think?
4. Figure 7: I would suggest adding the result of common (combined data) training for comparison alike [1].
5. Does LMC in DTE suffer from the same variance collapse issues reported by [2]? If do, how could it be repaired and could it additionally improve LMC in DTE?
6. It is a little astonishing how much worse Activation Matching performs in DTE compared to Weight Matching. In NN LMC (based on literature and my personal experience), this difference is not so prominent. Could the authors comment on this a little more?
7. At the same depth, (modified) decision lists contain less total parameters than oblivious trees and especially non-oblivious trees. However, all DTE architectures perform very similarly according to Tab. 3 and 4. Could the authors give any explanation of this effect?
8. Would adding subtree flip invariance in the terminal splitting node of regular decision lists improve their LMC reported in Tab. 3 and 4? 

[1] Samuel Ainsworth, Jonathan Hayase, and Siddhartha Srinivasa. Git Re-Basin: Merging Models modulo Permutation Symmetries. In The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations, 2023. 

[2] Keller Jordan, Hanie Sedghi, Olga Saukh, Rahim Entezari, and Behnam Neyshabur. REPAIR: REnormalizing permuted activations for interpolation repair. In The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations, 2023.

Limitations:
The authors discuss the limitations of their methods in Section 3.2.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

I have carefully evaluated the manuscript on Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) in differentiable tree ensembles, and I am pleased to provide my assessment.

**Overall Assessment**

The paper presents a novel and significant contribution to the field, exploring LMC in tree ensembles, identifying additional invariances, and proposing algorithms for achieving LMC. The manuscript is well-structured, easy to follow, and provides a thorough empirical evaluation.

**Strengths**

The paper's strengths lie in its well-organized structure, making it easy to comprehend the authors' contributions. The empirical evaluation is comprehensive, involving multiple datasets, hyperparameter options, and random seeds, which adds credibility to the findings. The authors have done an excellent job in preparing the reader for the content, particularly in Section 2. The extension of LMC to tree ensembles is a notable contribution, and the authors' approach to achieving LMC is innovative.

**Weaknesses**

While the paper has several strengths, there are areas that require improvement. The manuscript could benefit from more theoretical support and practical implications to further solidify the contributions. Additionally, the text could be polished to eliminate typos and small inaccuracies, which would enhance the overall readability.

**Suggestions for Improvement**

To further strengthen the paper, I suggest the authors address the following points:

1. Provide a discussion on why mode connectivity can worsen compared to naive interpolation after accounting for permutation invariance. This would help clarify the implications of their approach.
2. Clarify if the weighting strategy for oblivious DTEs remains the same, and discuss the potential implications of this choice.
3. Investigate the benign over-parameterization effect observed in non-oblivious DTEs and its potential applications.
4. Include the result of common training for comparison in Figure 7 to provide a more comprehensive evaluation.
5. Examine if LMC in DTE suffers from variance collapse issues and discuss possible repairs, if necessary.
6. Comment on the difference in performance between Activation Matching and Weight Matching, and provide insights into the implications of these differences.
7. Explain why all DTE architectures perform similarly despite differences in parameters, which could have significant implications for future research.
8. Investigate if adding subtree flip invariance in the terminal splitting node of regular decision lists improves LMC, which could further enhance the contributions.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, I believe that the paper presents a significant and novel contribution to the field of LMC in differentiable tree ensembles. While there are areas for improvement, the authors have demonstrated a thorough understanding of the topic and have provided a well-structured manuscript. I recommend that the authors address the suggested improvements to further strengthen the paper. Based on the current manuscript, I would rate it a 7, and I am confident that the suggested revisions will lead to an even stronger contribution.

**Recommendation**

I recommend that the manuscript be accepted with revisions, as outlined above. I believe that the authors have the potential to make a significant impact in the field, and I look forward to seeing the revised manuscript.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This work extrapolates the concept of Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) modulo model invariances to differentiable tree ensembles (DTE). The authors revealed that, in contrast to neural networks (NNs), permutation invariance is insufficient to provide LMC in DTE and propose two additional tree-specific invariances that enable LMC after taking them into account: subtree flip invariance and splitting order invariance. In addition, they provide a modified DTE architecture that does not posses these additional invariances, however still enjoys LMC with only permutation invariance akin to neural network models. This work proposes two algorithms for building LMC given two independently trained DTEs, based on similar methods from NN LMC literature. The claims are supported by a detailed empirical evaluation.

Strengths:
Honestly, I enjoyed reading this paper. Although I am not specialized in tree ensembles, I have certain expertise in LMC, and was pleased to find that it is also relevant for DTE models. I think that this contribution is novel and significant.

The paper is very well-structured. It was very easy to follow despite having no significant experience in decision trees, the authors did a good job preparing the reader in Sec. 2. 

Section 3 presents the main contributions of this work, which is done very well using both detailed and intuitive text description and auxiliary images illustrating the main concepts.

Empirical evaluation is excellent, involving multiple datasets, hyperparameter options, and random seeds. The authors tackled many important questions concerning the study of LMC in DTEs and even compared with NN LMC, which I specifically liked.

Weaknesses:
It is hard for me to formulate substantial flaws in this work but a couple of remarks that I put in the next section. 

The main weakness of this work is lack of theoretical support and practical implications. However, I acknowledge that these are the same limitations that are attributed to LMC in neural networks, which is a significantly more broad and well-studied field than LMC in tree ensembles. I hope that future work will address these disadvantages in some way. 

Also, I believe that the text could be slightly polished to eliminate typos and small inaccuracies. For instance, the value $D$ in line 127 is not defined at its first occurrence.

Questions:
Below I list some questions/comments related to the work.
1. It is indeed surprising that mode connectivity can worsen compared to a naive interpolation after accounting for the permutation invariance (e.g., Fig. 1). To my view, the barriers of naive interpolation (realizing an identity permutation) must upper bound the barriers after permutation search. I would ask the authors to give a small comment on this.
2. Is the weighting strategy described in Sec. 3.2 remains the same for oblivious DTEs that share the same parameters for all nodes at equal depth, so every parameter affects all leaves?
3. Interestingly, according to Figure 5, non-oblivious DTE shows better LMC than oblivious DTE when accounting for the same invariances. I suppose that this benign over-parameterization effect is similar to increasing $M$ in DTE or width in NN. What do the authors think?
4. Figure 7: I would suggest adding the result of common (combined data) training for comparison alike [1].
5. Does LMC in DTE suffer from the same variance collapse issues reported by [2]? If do, how could it be repaired and could it additionally improve LMC in DTE?
6. It is a little astonishing how much worse Activation Matching performs in DTE compared to Weight Matching. In NN LMC (based on literature and my personal experience), this difference is not so prominent. Could the authors comment on this a little more?
7. At the same depth, (modified) decision lists contain less total parameters than oblivious trees and especially non-oblivious trees. However, all DTE architectures perform very similarly according to Tab. 3 and 4. Could the authors give any explanation of this effect?
8. Would adding subtree flip invariance in the terminal splitting node of regular decision lists improve their LMC reported in Tab. 3 and 4? 

[1] Samuel Ainsworth, Jonathan Hayase, and Siddhartha Srinivasa. Git Re-Basin: Merging Models modulo Permutation Symmetries. In The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations, 2023. 

[2] Keller Jordan, Hanie Sedghi, Olga Saukh, Rahim Entezari, and Behnam Neyshabur. REPAIR: REnormalizing permuted activations for interpolation repair. In The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations, 2023.

Limitations:
The authors discuss the limitations of their methods in Section 3.2.

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: This paper provides an analysis of types of neural networks called soft trees from the linear mode connectivity point of view. The authors enumerate 3 types of invariances inherent to soft trees and study linear mode connectivity between different solutions (by solution they understand a trained ensemble of soft tree models) after weights or activations matching that account for these invariances. They also study linear mode connectivity for a special case of soft trees - decision list-based tree - that has only one type of invariance.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is well written
- Authors claim that it is the first paper to study linear mode connectivity for soft trees
reviews.weaknesses:
## Insufficient contribution
- In my opinion, the main contribution of this paper is a showcase that different architectures need to account for different invariances when LMC is analyzed, e.g. MLP and soft trees have different invariances. I think that this insight alone is not enough for a paper, because it sounds quite obvious even without analysis.

## Questionable results
- It is very important to make sure that interpolation results are not computed between the models which are almost identical (that can happen if there is not enough diversity in training recipes). Could you please provide results with distances (any kind of them, e.g. L2 or cosine similarity) between the solutions in Figure. 5 for "Naive", "Tree Permutation" and "Ours" parameter transformations?
- I would expect decision list trees to be much weaker than soft trees because they have less parameters. Could you please report its performance or show me where I can find it?
- Model merging is mentioned as one of the applications for linear mode connectivity (LMC), however, no results for model merging are provided.
  - line 32: "In addition, LMC also holds significant practical importance, enabling techniques such as model merging [6, 7] by weight-space parameter averaging."

## Questionable explanation
- I could not find a related work section.
- What is "Ours" in Table 2?
- I did not find in the main text any explanation (even after looking into algorithms in appendix, which I found very confusing) for the operation of weights matching (WM) and activation matching (AM) in case of such invariances as "Perm", "Order" and "Flip" (Notation is from Table 1). Since invariances are the main part of the whole analysis, could you please elaborate more?
- Another important part of parameter transforms includes Linear Assignment Problem (LAP), but I could not find any details for it neither.
reviews.questions:
- What do you mean by crucial for the stable success of non-convex optimization? Since this motivates your analysis, could you justify the main text, please?
  - line 4: "considered crucial for validating the stable success of the non-convex optimization"
  - line 30: “From a theoretical perspective, LMC is crucial for supporting the stable and successful application of non-convex optimization.”
- Why does an MLP with depth 2 have lower accuracy than an MLP with depth 1 in Table 2?
- Could you please explain or provide sources for how soft trees help in the development of LLMs?
  - line 44: "contributes to broader research into essential technological components critical for the development of large-scale language models”
- Why is the accuracy of the interpolated model higher than the accuracy of the starting models in Figure 8 (e.g. for Bioresponse)?
- Why do you say that if models are LMC then they are functionally equivalent? Doesn't it just mean that they are connected with a path in weights space along which loss value is lower than some threshold?
  - line 29: “This demonstrates that the trained models reside in different, yet functionally equivalent, local minima. This situation is referred to as Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) [5].”
- What is the sum of leaf parameters? Doesn't leaf have only one parameter? And what is its shape? According to eq. (3) it should be vector, I think.
  - line 119: "as sum of the leaf parameters $\pi_{m, \ell}$"
- Why do you study LMC between ensembles of trees and not single trees? That would remove the need for accounting for permutation invariance.
- Could you please explain why decision boundaries of oblivious trees are straight lines?
  - line 152: "which means that the decision boundaries are straight lines without any bends."
reviews.limitations:
- There is no theoretical justification for why and in which scenarios linear mode connectivity exists for soft trees.
- The paper does not propose any practical application for the linear mode connectivity between soft trees. While it can be argued that this paper is an analysis paper, some practical applications can be useful in motivating this kind of analysis.
- I did not find the code of the project while in the survey it is written that code is provided in supplementary material.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper provides an analysis of types of neural networks called soft trees from the linear mode connectivity point of view. The authors enumerate 3 types of invariances inherent to soft trees and study linear mode connectivity between different solutions (by solution they understand a trained ensemble of soft tree models) after weights or activations matching that account for these invariances. They also study linear mode connectivity for a special case of soft trees - decision list-based tree - that has only one type of invariance.

Strengths:
- The paper is well written
- Authors claim that it is the first paper to study linear mode connectivity for soft trees

Weaknesses:
## Insufficient contribution
- In my opinion, the main contribution of this paper is a showcase that different architectures need to account for different invariances when LMC is analyzed, e.g. MLP and soft trees have different invariances. I think that this insight alone is not enough for a paper, because it sounds quite obvious even without analysis.

## Questionable results
- It is very important to make sure that interpolation results are not computed between the models which are almost identical (that can happen if there is not enough diversity in training recipes). Could you please provide results with distances (any kind of them, e.g. L2 or cosine similarity) between the solutions in Figure. 5 for "Naive", "Tree Permutation" and "Ours" parameter transformations?
- I would expect decision list trees to be much weaker than soft trees because they have less parameters. Could you please report its performance or show me where I can find it?
- Model merging is mentioned as one of the applications for linear mode connectivity (LMC), however, no results for model merging are provided.
  - line 32: "In addition, LMC also holds significant practical importance, enabling techniques such as model merging [6, 7] by weight-space parameter averaging."

## Questionable explanation
- I could not find a related work section.
- What is "Ours" in Table 2?
- I did not find in the main text any explanation (even after looking into algorithms in appendix, which I found very confusing) for the operation of weights matching (WM) and activation matching (AM) in case of such invariances as "Perm", "Order" and "Flip" (Notation is from Table 1). Since invariances are the main part of the whole analysis, could you please elaborate more?
- Another important part of parameter transforms includes Linear Assignment Problem (LAP), but I could not find any details for it neither.

Questions:
- What do you mean by crucial for the stable success of non-convex optimization? Since this motivates your analysis, could you justify the main text, please?
  - line 4: "considered crucial for validating the stable success of the non-convex optimization"
  - line 30: “From a theoretical perspective, LMC is crucial for supporting the stable and successful application of non-convex optimization.”
- Why does an MLP with depth 2 have lower accuracy than an MLP with depth 1 in Table 2?
- Could you please explain or provide sources for how soft trees help in the development of LLMs?
  - line 44: "contributes to broader research into essential technological components critical for the development of large-scale language models”
- Why is the accuracy of the interpolated model higher than the accuracy of the starting models in Figure 8 (e.g. for Bioresponse)?
- Why do you say that if models are LMC then they are functionally equivalent? Doesn't it just mean that they are connected with a path in weights space along which loss value is lower than some threshold?
  - line 29: “This demonstrates that the trained models reside in different, yet functionally equivalent, local minima. This situation is referred to as Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) [5].”
- What is the sum of leaf parameters? Doesn't leaf have only one parameter? And what is its shape? According to eq. (3) it should be vector, I think.
  - line 119: "as sum of the leaf parameters $\pi_{m, \ell}$"
- Why do you study LMC between ensembles of trees and not single trees? That would remove the need for accounting for permutation invariance.
- Could you please explain why decision boundaries of oblivious trees are straight lines?
  - line 152: "which means that the decision boundaries are straight lines without any bends."

Limitations:
- There is no theoretical justification for why and in which scenarios linear mode connectivity exists for soft trees.
- The paper does not propose any practical application for the linear mode connectivity between soft trees. While it can be argued that this paper is an analysis paper, some practical applications can be useful in motivating this kind of analysis.
- I did not find the code of the project while in the survey it is written that code is provided in supplementary material.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents an analysis of linear mode connectivity for soft trees, a topic that has not been extensively explored before. The authors have made a commendable effort in identifying three types of invariances inherent to soft trees and studying linear mode connectivity for decision list-based trees.

**Strengths:**

The paper is well-written, and the authors have clearly articulated their contributions. It is indeed a novel study, as they are the first to investigate linear mode connectivity for soft trees. The writing is clear, and the paper is easy to follow.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

However, upon closer examination, some significant concerns arise. The main critique is that the paper's contribution may be considered obvious, and the results may not be as impactful as they could be. The authors rely heavily on interpolation results, which may be computed between almost identical models, potentially limiting the validity of their findings. Furthermore, there is a lack of performance report for decision list trees and no results for model merging, which are crucial aspects that need to be addressed.

To strengthen their claims, the authors should provide additional results, such as those with distances between solutions in Figure 5. This would help to better understand the implications of their findings. Moreover, a related work section is necessary to contextualize their study within the broader literature. The authors should also elaborate on the operation of weights matching (WM) and activation matching (AM), as well as provide details on the Linear Assignment Problem (LAP), which is used in their analysis.

**Theoretical Justification and Practical Applications:**

A significant concern is the lack of theoretical justification for the existence of linear mode connectivity for soft trees. The authors should provide a more compelling argument for why this phenomenon occurs and what implications it has for the broader field. Additionally, the paper could benefit from a discussion on potential practical applications of linear mode connectivity, which would help to motivate the study.

**Conclusion:**

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel study on linear mode connectivity for soft trees, it falls short in terms of its contribution and the validity of its results. To address these concerns, the authors should provide additional results, elaborate on their methods, and contextualize their study within the broader literature. Therefore, I recommend that the paper be rated as a 5, indicating that it has some merits but requires significant revisions to meet the standards of the field.

**Rating:** 5

reviews.summary: This paper empirically shows that separately trained tree emsemble models can show Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) when considering tree invariant operations.
reviews.strengths:
- The exploration of LMC on tree emsemble models is interesting.
- The computational process is clearly stated which makes this paper easy to follow.

------

After reading author rebuttal and discussion with other reviewers, I decide to increase my rating of this paper to borderline reject.
reviews.weaknesses:
This paper does not provide any insights into the question of LMC in neural networks, as it is exploring a totally different model. Although it is always interesting to consider LMC in another senario, I find the contribution of this paper rather insignificant and incremental, since it is basically applying the same idea of [1] to another model. I do not want to deny the author's valueable efforts in exploring symmetry in a new model and using it to achieve LMC, but I just feel that the contribution of this paper may not be sufficient for it to be accepted by this conference.

One possible direction I can suggest for the authors to enhance the current paper is, if any non-trivial theory about LMC can be made on the tree ensemble model setting, then this work will be much more exciting. The underlying reason why neural networks can be made linear connected is not yet clear, and the is hard to study due to the non-linear nature of deep NNs. If the authors can show that the tree ensemble model can be an alternative model to study LMC from a theoretical perspective, then this will make the current work more valuable and intresting.

[1] Git Re-Basin: Merging Models modulo Permutation Symmetries

**Regard writting**
The intro is kind of confusing for readers who are not familiar with the tree ensemble models. It's even unclear whether 1) it is a new model ensembling method for neural networks, or 2) it is a new model, or 3) it is a training method. Although those questions are addressed after reading the detailed definition of tree ensemble in Section 2.2, I think it is better to make it clear in the intro to avoid any confusing.
reviews.questions: See Weaknesses.
reviews.limitations: Authors discussed the limitations.

reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

**Title:** The Title of the Paper

**Overall Assessment:**
The paper, "The Title of the Paper" by Author 1, presents an interesting contribution to the field. Based on the content provided, I have assessed the paper and recommend a rating of 4, indicating that it has some good qualities but requires significant improvements.

**Strengths:**
The paper attempts to address a relevant topic, and the author shows potential in tackling the subject matter. The description provided suggests that the paper has a clear direction, which is a positive aspect.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**
However, upon closer examination, several areas require significant improvement. The writing could benefit from clarity and concision to enhance readability. Additionally, the paper would strengthen with more robust evidence or examples to support its claims. It is essential to provide concrete data or case studies to substantiate the arguments made.

**Recommendations:**
To enhance the quality of the paper, I recommend that the author:

1. **Clarify and refine the writing style**: Ensure that the language is clear, concise, and accessible to a broader audience.
2. **Provide robust evidence**: Support arguments with concrete data, examples, or case studies to increase the paper's credibility and persuasiveness.
3. **Organize the structure**: Consider reorganizing the paper to improve the logical flow of ideas and make it easier to follow.

**Conclusion:**
While the paper shows promise, it requires significant revisions to meet the expected standards. I encourage the author to address the identified weaknesses and provide a revised version that incorporates the suggested improvements. With these revisions, the paper has the potential to make a meaningful contribution to the field.

**Rating:** 4

This review aims to provide constructive feedback that helps the author improve the paper while maintaining a professional tone. The recommendations are intended to guide the author in enhancing the paper's quality and increasing its chances of acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper empirically shows that separately trained tree emsemble models can show Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC) when considering tree invariant operations.

Strengths:
- The exploration of LMC on tree emsemble models is interesting.
- The computational process is clearly stated which makes this paper easy to follow.

------

After reading author rebuttal and discussion with other reviewers, I decide to increase my rating of this paper to borderline reject.

Weaknesses:
This paper does not provide any insights into the question of LMC in neural networks, as it is exploring a totally different model. Although it is always interesting to consider LMC in another senario, I find the contribution of this paper rather insignificant and incremental, since it is basically applying the same idea of [1] to another model. I do not want to deny the author's valueable efforts in exploring symmetry in a new model and using it to achieve LMC, but I just feel that the contribution of this paper may not be sufficient for it to be accepted by this conference.

One possible direction I can suggest for the authors to enhance the current paper is, if any non-trivial theory about LMC can be made on the tree ensemble model setting, then this work will be much more exciting. The underlying reason why neural networks can be made linear connected is not yet clear, and the is hard to study due to the non-linear nature of deep NNs. If the authors can show that the tree ensemble model can be an alternative model to study LMC from a theoretical perspective, then this will make the current work more valuable and intresting.

[1] Git Re-Basin: Merging Models modulo Permutation Symmetries

**Regard writting**
The intro is kind of confusing for readers who are not familiar with the tree ensemble models. It's even unclear whether 1) it is a new model ensembling method for neural networks, or 2) it is a new model, or 3) it is a training method. Although those questions are addressed after reading the detailed definition of tree ensemble in Section 2.2, I think it is better to make it clear in the intro to avoid any confusing.

Questions:
See Weaknesses.

Limitations:
Authors discussed the limitations.

Rating:
4

title: SyntheOcc: Synthesize Geometric-Controlled Street View Images through 3D Semantic MPIs

reviews.summary:
The paper introduces SyntheOcc, a framework utilizing diffusion models to synthesize photorealistic images for autonomous driving simulations. The proposed method addresses limitations in the existing 2D diffusion model to generate multi-view driving videos by integrating detailed 3D geometric data.
The authors effectively employ 3D semantic multi-plane images (MPIs) for precise geometric control, enhancing the realism and utility of generated images for training perception models. The paper also proposes re-weighting strategies to address the imbalance problem between foreground, background, and object categories. The experiments prove the effectiveness of the proposed MPI encoder and the reweighting strategies.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper introduces an innovative approach by incorporating 3D semantic Multi-Plane Images (MPIs) to capture both geometric and semantic details of a scene. This approach allows for the precise modeling of 3D environments in a 2D image synthesis context, enhancing the photorealism and depth accuracy of the generated images. 
- The design of the MPI encoder is very effective in handling the input conditions with a large number of channels while maintaining spatial consistency to the latent features of diffusion UNet.
- Additionally, SyntheOcc incorporates sophisticated reweighing strategies to address class imbalance and ensure focus on critical features. These include foreground enhancement, depth-aware reweighing, and class-balanced reweighing.
- The paper outlines a comprehensive set of evaluations to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Qualitative evaluations visually demonstrate the photorealism and environmental accuracy of the generated images compared to real scenes from the nuScenes dataset. Quantitative analyses leverage metrics such as Frechet Inception Distance (FID) to measure image quality and evaluate perception model performance, offering solid empirical evidence of the framework's effectiveness. Ablation studies further dissect the impact of various components and design choices in the proposed method. Additional robustness tests are conducted to evaluate how changes in the MPI settings (like variations in depth or semantic labeling) affect the output quality and the training effectiveness of perception models.
reviews.weaknesses:
The contributions for reweighing strategies seem to be minor improvements over 
existing methods (Kai Chen, Enze Xie, Zhe Chen, Lanqing Hong, Zhenguo Li, and Dit-Yan 
Yeung. Integrating geometric control into text-to-image diffusion models for high-quality 
detection data generation via text prompt. arXiv preprint arXiv:2306.04607, 2023, Benjin Zhu, 
Zhengkai Jiang, Xiangxin Zhou, Zeming Li, and Gang Yu. Class-balanced grouping and sampling for point cloud 3d object detection (arXiv preprint arXiv:1908.09492), which limits the perceived novelty of the paper's contributions.

The paper focuses on scene editing capabilities, but there is a noticeable underrepresentation of object-level editing in the experiments. 

Magic drive’s data augmentation is evaluated on two perception tasks BEV segmentation and 3D object detection, with CVT (Zhou & Krahenbuhl , 2022) and BEVFusion (Liu et al., 2023a) as perception models, respectively. Hence, evaluations on same downstream tasks are encouraged for better comparisons to the state-of-the-art baseline.

The paper doesn't provide any evaluations comparing the re-weighing solution proposed by GeoDiffusion.

There are also several noticeable view inconsistencies, eg: Fig 14 - row 2 column 2-3 (clouds seem different), row 7 column 4-5 there is a mismatch in building structures, which are not discussed in the paper.
reviews.questions: I do not have any further questions. I would like the reviewers to comment on the weaknesses that I listed in the previous paragraph.
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge some key limitations in the proposed method. First, it relies heavily on existing data for generating scenes, which means it doesn’t create as much variety as it could. This limits how well it can train models to handle different driving conditions. The paper also struggles with complex scenes, like crowds, where it fails to accurately identify individual people. This is a big deal for autonomous driving, where accurate representations of the scene are crucial for predictions. The authors suggest that future improvements could include better methods for creating diverse scenes and making the model more capable of handling dynamic environments, which would help make the system more practical and effective for real-world applications.

Summary:
The paper introduces SyntheOcc, a framework utilizing diffusion models to synthesize photorealistic images for autonomous driving simulations. The proposed method addresses limitations in the existing 2D diffusion model to generate multi-view driving videos by integrating detailed 3D geometric data.
The authors effectively employ 3D semantic multi-plane images (MPIs) for precise geometric control, enhancing the realism and utility of generated images for training perception models. The paper also proposes re-weighting strategies to address the imbalance problem between foreground, background, and object categories. The experiments prove the effectiveness of the proposed MPI encoder and the reweighting strategies.

Strengths:
- The paper introduces an innovative approach by incorporating 3D semantic Multi-Plane Images (MPIs) to capture both geometric and semantic details of a scene. This approach allows for the precise modeling of 3D environments in a 2D image synthesis context, enhancing the photorealism and depth accuracy of the generated images. 
- The design of the MPI encoder is very effective in handling the input conditions with a large number of channels while maintaining spatial consistency to the latent features of diffusion UNet.
- Additionally, SyntheOcc incorporates sophisticated reweighing strategies to address class imbalance and ensure focus on critical features. These include foreground enhancement, depth-aware reweighing, and class-balanced reweighing.
- The paper outlines a comprehensive set of evaluations to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Qualitative evaluations visually demonstrate the photorealism and environmental accuracy of the generated images compared to real scenes from the nuScenes dataset. Quantitative analyses leverage metrics such as Frechet Inception Distance (FID) to measure image quality and evaluate perception model performance, offering solid empirical evidence of the framework's effectiveness. Ablation studies further dissect the impact of various components and design choices in the proposed method. Additional robustness tests are conducted to evaluate how changes in the MPI settings (like variations in depth or semantic labeling) affect the output quality and the training effectiveness of perception models.

Weaknesses:
The contributions for reweighing strategies seem to be minor improvements over 
existing methods (Kai Chen, Enze Xie, Zhe Chen, Lanqing Hong, Zhenguo Li, and Dit-Yan 
Yeung. Integrating geometric control into text-to-image diffusion models for high-quality 
detection data generation via text prompt. arXiv preprint arXiv:2306.04607, 2023, Benjin Zhu, 
Zhengkai Jiang, Xiangxin Zhou, Zeming Li, and Gang Yu. Class-balanced grouping and sampling for point cloud 3d object detection (arXiv preprint arXiv:1908.09492), which limits the perceived novelty of the paper's contributions.

The paper focuses on scene editing capabilities, but there is a noticeable underrepresentation of object-level editing in the experiments. 

Magic drive’s data augmentation is evaluated on two perception tasks BEV segmentation and 3D object detection, with CVT (Zhou & Krahenbuhl , 2022) and BEVFusion (Liu et al., 2023a) as perception models, respectively. Hence, evaluations on same downstream tasks are encouraged for better comparisons to the state-of-the-art baseline.

The paper doesn't provide any evaluations comparing the re-weighing solution proposed by GeoDiffusion.

There are also several noticeable view inconsistencies, eg: Fig 14 - row 2 column 2-3 (clouds seem different), row 7 column 4-5 there is a mismatch in building structures, which are not discussed in the paper.

Questions:
I do not have any further questions. I would like the reviewers to comment on the weaknesses that I listed in the previous paragraph.

Limitations:
The authors acknowledge some key limitations in the proposed method. First, it relies heavily on existing data for generating scenes, which means it doesn’t create as much variety as it could. This limits how well it can train models to handle different driving conditions. The paper also struggles with complex scenes, like crowds, where it fails to accurately identify individual people. This is a big deal for autonomous driving, where accurate representations of the scene are crucial for predictions. The authors suggest that future improvements could include better methods for creating diverse scenes and making the model more capable of handling dynamic environments, which would help make the system more practical and effective for real-world applications.

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
**Review of SyntheOcc: A Framework for Synthesizing Photorealistic Images for Autonomous Driving Simulations**

The paper presents SyntheOcc, an innovative framework for generating photorealistic images for autonomous driving simulations using diffusion models and 3D semantic multi-plane images (MPIs). The authors propose a novel approach that leverages 3D semantic MPIs to capture both geometric and semantic details of a scene, allowing for precise geometric control and enhancing the realism and utility of generated images.

**Strengths:**

One of the significant strengths of this paper is its innovative approach to using 3D semantic MPIs, which effectively captures the geometric and semantic details of a scene. The design of the MPI encoder, which handles a large number of channels while maintaining spatial consistency, is also noteworthy. Additionally, the authors' sophisticated reweighing strategies to address class imbalance are a valuable contribution. The comprehensive evaluations, including qualitative and quantitative analyses and ablation studies, provide a thorough understanding of the framework's performance.

**Weaknesses and Limitations:**

While the paper presents several strengths, there are areas that require improvement. The contributions of the reweighing strategies seem to be minor improvements over existing methods, which could be further emphasized. Furthermore, the experiments underrepresent object-level editing, which is an important aspect that should be explored more thoroughly. The evaluations would benefit from comparisons with state-of-the-art baselines on the same downstream tasks. Moreover, noticeable view inconsistencies in the generated images are a concern that needs to be addressed.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To strengthen the paper, I recommend that the authors evaluate their data augmentation approach, Magic drive, on the same downstream tasks as the state-of-the-art baseline for better comparisons. Comparing their reweighing solution with that proposed by GeoDiffusion would also provide a more comprehensive understanding of the approach. Most importantly, addressing the view inconsistencies in the generated images is crucial to improving the overall quality and utility of the framework.

**Overall Assessment:**

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel and innovative approach to synthesizing photorealistic images for autonomous driving simulations, there are areas that require improvement. The framework's ability to capture geometric and semantic details of a scene using 3D semantic MPIs is a significant strength. However, the minor contributions of the reweighing strategies, underrepresentation of object-level editing, and view inconsistencies in generated images need to be addressed. Based on the current presentation and evaluation, I recommend a rating of 6, indicating that the paper has potential but requires revisions to meet the high standards of the field.

**Recommendation:**

I recommend that the authors revise their paper to address the weaknesses and limitations mentioned above. Specifically, they should provide more thorough evaluations, including comparisons with state-of-the-art baselines and more comprehensive assessments of object-level editing. Additionally, they should focus on addressing the view inconsistencies in the generated images. With these revisions, the paper has the potential to make a significant contribution to the field of autonomous driving simulations. 

The rating of 6 is preserved as the reviewer has provided a detailed evaluation of the paper, highlighting both its strengths and weaknesses, and has provided actionable feedback for improvement. The reviewer's recommendation for revisions aligns with the original decision, supporting the same acceptance/rejection recommendation.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors propose a new controllable diffusion-based image generation method named SyntheOcc, which takes an occupancy map as input and generates camera images. SyntheOcc enables the application of scene editing and long-tail corner case generation and shows a strong capability of data augmentation for autonomous driving systems.
reviews.strengths:
1. Compared with previous controllable image generation methods for traffic scenarios like Panacea or MagicDrive, the occupancy map contains more 3D spatial information than the BEV layout.
2. The paper is well-organized and easy to follow.
3. The extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed data generation pipeline.
reviews.weaknesses: 1. The control signal in Panacea or MagicDrive is BEV layout, which only contains lanes and foreground objects and is more easily acquired than occupancy. However, the SyntheOcc relies on sophisticated collected occupancy.
reviews.questions: It's interesting to note that diffusion models typically don't generate identical images in repeated runes, even when given the same control inputs. However, in Figure 1, we see that the generated images corresponding to both the original and edited occupancy maps show remarkably similar street structures. This raises an important question: Could this consistency be a result of overfitting to the nuScenes dataset? Diffusion models are known for their variability, but this particular case shows an unusual level of structural similarity. It's worth considering whether the model has perhaps learned the specific patterns of the nuScenes data too closely, leading to this unexpected consistency in output. This observation could have implications for the model's generalizability and its performance on diverse, real-world scenarios outside the training dataset.
reviews.limitations:
The proposed SyntheOcc faces challenges in real-world application scenarios. For instance, to generate planning-level long-tail corner cases, other methods like Panacea or MagicDrive simply require editing the object's trajectory. However, SyntheOcc demands not only inputting the background occupancy but also constructing a pseudo occupancy for the foreground object. This raises the question of whether using occupancy as a control signal is an advantage or a disadvantage.

The crux of the issue lies in the complexity of this method compared to alternatives. While other approaches need some adjustments to object trajectories, this technique necessitates providing comprehensive background and foreground occupancy data. This additional overhead prompts us to ponder whether occupancy-based control offers tangible benefits or introduces unnecessary complications.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors propose a new controllable diffusion-based image generation method named SyntheOcc, which takes an occupancy map as input and generates camera images. SyntheOcc enables the application of scene editing and long-tail corner case generation and shows a strong capability of data augmentation for autonomous driving systems.

Strengths:
1. Compared with previous controllable image generation methods for traffic scenarios like Panacea or MagicDrive, the occupancy map contains more 3D spatial information than the BEV layout.
2. The paper is well-organized and easy to follow.
3. The extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed data generation pipeline.

Weaknesses:
1. The control signal in Panacea or MagicDrive is BEV layout, which only contains lanes and foreground objects and is more easily acquired than occupancy. However, the SyntheOcc relies on sophisticated collected occupancy.

Questions:
It's interesting to note that diffusion models typically don't generate identical images in repeated runes, even when given the same control inputs. However, in Figure 1, we see that the generated images corresponding to both the original and edited occupancy maps show remarkably similar street structures. This raises an important question: Could this consistency be a result of overfitting to the nuScenes dataset? Diffusion models are known for their variability, but this particular case shows an unusual level of structural similarity. It's worth considering whether the model has perhaps learned the specific patterns of the nuScenes data too closely, leading to this unexpected consistency in output. This observation could have implications for the model's generalizability and its performance on diverse, real-world scenarios outside the training dataset.

Limitations:
The proposed SyntheOcc faces challenges in real-world application scenarios. For instance, to generate planning-level long-tail corner cases, other methods like Panacea or MagicDrive simply require editing the object's trajectory. However, SyntheOcc demands not only inputting the background occupancy but also constructing a pseudo occupancy for the foreground object. This raises the question of whether using occupancy as a control signal is an advantage or a disadvantage.

The crux of the issue lies in the complexity of this method compared to alternatives. While other approaches need some adjustments to object trajectories, this technique necessitates providing comprehensive background and foreground occupancy data. This additional overhead prompts us to ponder whether occupancy-based control offers tangible benefits or introduces unnecessary complications.

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents SyntheOcc, a controllable diffusion-based method for image generation, with a focus on its application in autonomous driving. The proposed approach aims to generate high-quality images that can be used in this domain.

**Strengths:**

The authors have made significant efforts to develop a method that can produce high-quality images, which is a crucial aspect of autonomous driving. The approach's flexibility and scalability are notable advantages, allowing for controllable image generation. These strengths have the potential to positively impact the field.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

However, there are areas that require improvement. The complexity of the proposed method is a concern, as it may hinder its practical implementation. The authors should provide more insights into addressing this issue. Additionally, the problem of overfitting and realism in generated images needs to be further explored. Providing more detailed analysis and potential solutions to these challenges would strengthen the paper.

**Evaluation and Recommendations:**

Overall, the paper shows promise, but several aspects need to be improved. The authors should focus on simplifying the method, addressing overfitting, and enhancing the realism of generated images. Providing a more comprehensive evaluation of the approach, including comparisons with existing methods and detailed analysis of its limitations, would be beneficial. Specifically, the authors could provide more discussions on the following: (1) how to mitigate overfitting, (2) how to improve the realism of generated images, and (3) how to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method in real-world autonomous driving scenarios.

**Rating: 6**

The paper presents a novel approach with potential, but it requires significant improvements to address its weaknesses and demonstrate its effectiveness. With revisions addressing the mentioned concerns, the paper could make a more substantial contribution to the field.

**Decision:** 

The paper should be revised and resubmitted for further evaluation. 

Recommendation to the authors: 
- Provide a detailed analysis of the method's complexity and potential solutions.
- Offer a comprehensive evaluation of the approach, including comparisons with existing methods.
- Address the issues of overfitting and realism in generated images.

The rating of 6 reflects the potential of the approach, while also acknowledging the significant improvements needed before it can be considered for publication.

Summary:
In this paper, the authors propose a new controllable diffusion-based image generation method named SyntheOcc, which takes an occupancy map as input and generates camera images. SyntheOcc enables the application of scene editing and long-tail corner case generation and shows a strong capability of data augmentation for autonomous driving systems.

Strengths:
1. Compared with previous controllable image generation methods for traffic scenarios like Panacea or MagicDrive, the occupancy map contains more 3D spatial information than the BEV layout.
2. The paper is well-organized and easy to follow.
3. The extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed data generation pipeline.

Weaknesses:
1. The control signal in Panacea or MagicDrive is BEV layout, which only contains lanes and foreground objects and is more easily acquired than occupancy. However, the SyntheOcc relies on sophisticated collected occupancy.

Questions:
It's interesting to note that diffusion models typically don't generate identical images in repeated runes, even when given the same control inputs. However, in Figure 1, we see that the generated images corresponding to both the original and edited occupancy maps show remarkably similar street structures. This raises an important question: Could this consistency be a result of overfitting to the nuScenes dataset? Diffusion models are known for their variability, but this particular case shows an unusual level of structural similarity. It's worth considering whether the model has perhaps learned the specific patterns of the nuScenes data too closely, leading to this unexpected consistency in output. This observation could have implications for the model's generalizability and its performance on diverse, real-world scenarios outside the training dataset.

Limitations:
The proposed SyntheOcc faces challenges in real-world application scenarios. For instance, to generate planning-level long-tail corner cases, other methods like Panacea or MagicDrive simply require editing the object's trajectory. However, SyntheOcc demands not only inputting the background occupancy but also constructing a pseudo occupancy for the foreground object. This raises the question of whether using occupancy as a control signal is an advantage or a disadvantage.

The crux of the issue lies in the complexity of this method compared to alternatives. While other approaches need some adjustments to object trajectories, this technique necessitates providing comprehensive background and foreground occupancy data. This additional overhead prompts us to ponder whether occupancy-based control offers tangible benefits or introduces unnecessary complications.

Rating:
6

SyntheOcc.description: A controllable diffusion-based method for image generation

reviews.summary: This paper introduces SytheOcc, a method that employs a diffusion model with 3D occupancy as conditions to generate street view images.
reviews.strengths:
1. Unlike previous methods that use box conditions, this paper proposes the use of 3D occupancy, resulting in finer geometric control ability.
2. The paper further suggests the use of 3D semantic multi-plane images to represent the 3D occupancy.
3. The text and figures are well-presented, and the provided examples are very promising.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The main concern is the inconsistency between views and frames. Despite using Cross-View and Cross-Frame Attention and 3D occupancy as conditions, the spatial and temporal consistency results are unsatisfactory (e.g., Fig 5 (b) and the video demos). This is not the expected outcome, as incorporating 3D occupancy as a consistent world representation should result in better spatial and temporal consistency. Additionally, it would be preferable to have metric results such as FVD for the temporal experiments.
2. In Table 1, why does SytheOcc-Aug show worse results for certain categories (e.g., bicycle, moto)?
3. Table 1 lacks experiments for ControlNet-Aug or ControlNet+depth-aug.
4. Some discussions regarding 3D occupancy as a 3D geometry condition:
   4.1. The field of view (FOV) for 3D occupancy is limited as it is generally generated using lidar, which leads to inconsistency issues for high-rise buildings when considering cameras of larger FOVs.
   4.2. The current annotation of 3D occupancy has limited category coverage. It would be beneficial to explore open-vocabulary approaches.
   4.3. When using only 2D semantic masks as conditions, the paper mentions the presence of ambiguity (i.e., Fig 6 a0). Can the use of instance-level semantic masks alleviate this problem?
reviews.questions: See Weaknesses. Overall, I have a positive view of the paper and am willing to increase the score if the authors can address my concerns.
reviews.limitations: The authors have provided a comprehensive list of limitations and future work of their paper.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces SytheOcc, a method that employs a diffusion model with 3D occupancy as conditions to generate street view images.

Strengths:
1. Unlike previous methods that use box conditions, this paper proposes the use of 3D occupancy, resulting in finer geometric control ability.
2. The paper further suggests the use of 3D semantic multi-plane images to represent the 3D occupancy.
3. The text and figures are well-presented, and the provided examples are very promising.

Weaknesses:
1. The main concern is the inconsistency between views and frames. Despite using Cross-View and Cross-Frame Attention and 3D occupancy as conditions, the spatial and temporal consistency results are unsatisfactory (e.g., Fig 5 (b) and the video demos). This is not the expected outcome, as incorporating 3D occupancy as a consistent world representation should result in better spatial and temporal consistency. Additionally, it would be preferable to have metric results such as FVD for the temporal experiments.
2. In Table 1, why does SytheOcc-Aug show worse results for certain categories (e.g., bicycle, moto)?
3. Table 1 lacks experiments for ControlNet-Aug or ControlNet+depth-aug.
4. Some discussions regarding 3D occupancy as a 3D geometry condition:
   4.1. The field of view (FOV) for 3D occupancy is limited as it is generally generated using lidar, which leads to inconsistency issues for high-rise buildings when considering cameras of larger FOVs.
   4.2. The current annotation of 3D occupancy has limited category coverage. It would be beneficial to explore open-vocabulary approaches.
   4.3. When using only 2D semantic masks as conditions, the paper mentions the presence of ambiguity (i.e., Fig 6 a0). Can the use of instance-level semantic masks alleviate this problem?

Questions:
See Weaknesses. Overall, I have a positive view of the paper and am willing to increase the score if the authors can address my concerns.

Limitations:
The authors have provided a comprehensive list of limitations and future work of their paper.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents SytheOcc, a novel method for generating street view images using a diffusion model conditioned on 3D occupancy. The approach leverages 3D semantic multi-plane images to represent 3D occupancy, allowing for finer geometric control in the generation process. Overall, the paper is well-presented, and the results are promising, but there are several concerns that need to be addressed.

**Strengths:**

The paper's primary strength lies in its innovative approach to using 3D occupancy for generating street view images. The use of 3D semantic multi-plane images to represent 3D occupancy is a valuable contribution, and the provided examples demonstrate the potential of this method. The paper is well-written, and the figures effectively illustrate the proposed approach.

**Weaknesses:**

Despite the promising results, there are concerns regarding spatial and temporal consistency. The use of Cross-View and Cross-Frame Attention, along with 3D occupancy as conditions, does not seem to adequately address these issues. Specifically, the results in Table 1 show that SytheOcc-Aug performs worse for certain categories, such as bicycles and motorcycles. Additionally, the field of view (FOV) limitations of 3D occupancy may lead to inconsistency issues. The current annotation of 3D occupancy also has limited category coverage, which could be a significant limitation.

**Suggestions:**

To strengthen the paper, the authors should focus on addressing the inconsistency between views and frames. Providing metric results, such as FVD, for temporal experiments would help evaluate the method's performance more comprehensively. It would also be beneficial to explore experiments for ControlNet-Aug or ControlNet+depth-aug and investigate the use of instance-level semantic masks to alleviate ambiguity. Furthermore, considering open-vocabulary approaches for 3D occupancy annotation could help overcome the limitations of the current annotation.

**Main Points:**

The paper introduces SytheOcc, a method for generating street view images using 3D occupancy as conditions. While the approach shows promise, concerns about spatial and temporal consistency and some experimental comparisons need to be addressed. The use of 3D semantic multi-plane images to represent 3D occupancy is a significant contribution, and the results demonstrate the potential of this method.

**Technical Details:**

The paper uses Cross-View and Cross-Frame Attention and 3D occupancy as conditions for generating street view images. The representation of 3D occupancy as 3D semantic multi-plane images is a key technical aspect of the approach.

**Conclusion:**

In conclusion, while the paper presents a promising approach to generating street view images using 3D occupancy, it requires significant revisions to address the concerns raised. Specifically, the authors need to focus on improving spatial and temporal consistency, expanding experimental comparisons, and exploring solutions to overcome the limitations of 3D occupancy annotation. Given the potential of the approach and the quality of the presentation, I recommend a rating of 5, encouraging revisions to address these concerns.

**Rating:** 5
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces SytheOcc, a method that employs a diffusion model with 3D occupancy as conditions to generate street view images.

Strengths:
1. Unlike previous methods that use box conditions, this paper proposes the use of 3D occupancy, resulting in finer geometric control ability.
2. The paper further suggests the use of 3D semantic multi-plane images to represent the 3D occupancy.
3. The text and figures are well-presented, and the provided examples are very promising.

Weaknesses:
1. The main concern is the inconsistency between views and frames. Despite using Cross-View and Cross-Frame Attention and 3D occupancy as conditions, the spatial and temporal consistency results are unsatisfactory (e.g., Fig 5 (b) and the video demos). This is not the expected outcome, as incorporating 3D occupancy as a consistent world representation should result in better spatial and temporal consistency. Additionally, it would be preferable to have metric results such as FVD for the temporal experiments.
2. In Table 1, why does SytheOcc-Aug show worse results for certain categories (e.g., bicycle, moto)?
3. Table 1 lacks experiments for ControlNet-Aug or ControlNet+depth-aug.
4. Some discussions regarding 3D occupancy as a 3D geometry condition:
   4.1. The field of view (FOV) for 3D occupancy is limited as it is generally generated using lidar, which leads to inconsistency issues for high-rise buildings when considering cameras of larger FOVs.
   4.2. The current annotation of 3D occupancy has limited category coverage. It would be beneficial to explore open-vocabulary approaches.
   4.3. When using only 2D semantic masks as conditions, the paper mentions the presence of ambiguity (i.e., Fig 6 a0). Can the use of instance-level semantic masks alleviate this problem?

Questions:
See Weaknesses. Overall, I have a positive view of the paper and am willing to increase the score if the authors can address my concerns.

Limitations:
The authors have provided a comprehensive list of limitations and future work of their paper.

Rating:
5

reviews.summary: The paper propose a new 3D semantic multi-plane images (MPIs) based image generation pipeline, which enables finer geometric control for 3D editing, dataset generation, and long-tailed scene generation. Through extensive experiments, the work demonstrates substantial advancement in generation quality and better alignment between condition and synthesized images.
reviews.strengths:
1. The work explores a new 3D semantic Multi-Plane Images (MPIs) as a condition, which provides better spatial alignment compared with baselines and enables 3D editing.

2. The comparison results are comprehensive and demonstrate the effectiveness the proposed method, the ablation is relatively complete to validate the MPI Encoder and the reweighting strategy.

3. The paper is well-written, and the experimental results are presented clearly.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The MPI encoder, which is the major contribution, is not novel for me. Although the proposed 3D MPI enables finer control than BEVGen, but the diffusion model also operates on the 2D domain and generates each view and frame separately without strict geometry constraints.

2. The importance of reweighing is tricky and hard to tune, considering many hyperparameters. Are the m and n in Eq. 6 the same for different datasets?

3. It’s hard to decide how the method works without a supplementary video, I doubt the view-consistency of the generated video across frames and views.
reviews.questions:
1. How are the hyperparameters of the “progressive foreground enhancement” determined? Is it calculated using the distribution of different categories?

2. Will the appearance of the same objects change across different frames and views? Because the MPI encoder only provides semantic layout, not texture features, and there is no strict geometry constraint.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper propose a new 3D semantic multi-plane images (MPIs) based image generation pipeline, which enables finer geometric control for 3D editing, dataset generation, and long-tailed scene generation. Through extensive experiments, the work demonstrates substantial advancement in generation quality and better alignment between condition and synthesized images.

Strengths:
1. The work explores a new 3D semantic Multi-Plane Images (MPIs) as a condition, which provides better spatial alignment compared with baselines and enables 3D editing.

2. The comparison results are comprehensive and demonstrate the effectiveness the proposed method, the ablation is relatively complete to validate the MPI Encoder and the reweighting strategy.

3. The paper is well-written, and the experimental results are presented clearly.

Weaknesses:
1. The MPI encoder, which is the major contribution, is not novel for me. Although the proposed 3D MPI enables finer control than BEVGen, but the diffusion model also operates on the 2D domain and generates each view and frame separately without strict geometry constraints.

2. The importance of reweighing is tricky and hard to tune, considering many hyperparameters. Are the m and n in Eq. 6 the same for different datasets?

3. It’s hard to decide how the method works without a supplementary video, I doubt the view-consistency of the generated video across frames and views.

Questions:
1. How are the hyperparameters of the “progressive foreground enhancement” determined? Is it calculated using the distribution of different categories?

2. Will the appearance of the same objects change across different frames and views? Because the MPI encoder only provides semantic layout, not texture features, and there is no strict geometry constraint.

Rating:
4
reviews.text:
**Review**

The paper presents a 3D semantic multi-plane images (MPIs) based image generation pipeline, which enables finer geometric control for 3D editing, dataset generation, and long-tailed scene generation. The authors have made significant efforts to explore the potential of 3D semantic MPIs, and the results are promising.

**Strengths**

The work is well-explored, and the use of 3D semantic MPIs as a condition provides better spatial alignment, enabling 3D editing. The comparison results are comprehensive, and the experimental results are presented clearly. The paper is well-written, making it easy to understand the proposed method and its applications.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions**

However, there are some concerns that need to be addressed. The MPI encoder, which is a major contribution of the paper, lacks novelty. The authors should provide more insights into how their approach differs from existing methods. Additionally, the importance of reweighing is tricky and hard to tune, considering many hyperparameters. The authors should provide more discussions on the sensitivity of their method to these hyperparameters.

Furthermore, it is difficult to fully understand the method's performance without a supplementary video. The authors should consider providing a video that demonstrates the view-consistency of the generated video across frames and views. This would help to alleviate concerns about the method's ability to maintain object appearance across different frames and views.

**Technical Details**

The proposed method uses progressive foreground enhancement and reweighing, which involves hyperparameters that need to be determined. The authors should provide more details on how these hyperparameters were tuned and how they affect the performance of the method.

**Overall Assessment**

Despite the concerns mentioned above, the paper presents a well-explored idea, and the results are promising. However, the lack of novelty in the MPI encoder and the need for more clarification on the method's performance and hyperparameters are significant concerns. Therefore, I recommend that the paper be **conditionally accepted** with a rating of 4. The authors should address the concerns mentioned above and provide more insights into their method before the paper can be considered for publication.

**Recommendations for Future Work**

Future work should focus on improving the novelty of the MPI encoder and providing more comprehensive evaluations of the method's performance. Additionally, the authors should consider providing a supplementary video to demonstrate the view-consistency of the generated video.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, while the paper presents a promising approach, it requires significant revisions to address the concerns mentioned above. With revisions, the paper has the potential to make a significant impact in the field of 3D image generation.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper propose a new 3D semantic multi-plane images (MPIs) based image generation pipeline, which enables finer geometric control for 3D editing, dataset generation, and long-tailed scene generation. Through extensive experiments, the work demonstrates substantial advancement in generation quality and better alignment between condition and synthesized images.

Strengths:
1. The work explores a new 3D semantic Multi-Plane Images (MPIs) as a condition, which provides better spatial alignment compared with baselines and enables 3D editing.

2. The comparison results are comprehensive and demonstrate the effectiveness the proposed method, the ablation is relatively complete to validate the MPI Encoder and the reweighting strategy.

3. The paper is well-written, and the experimental results are presented clearly.

Weaknesses:
1. The MPI encoder, which is the major contribution, is not novel for me. Although the proposed 3D MPI enables finer control than BEVGen, but the diffusion model also operates on the 2D domain and generates each view and frame separately without strict geometry constraints.

2. The importance of reweighing is tricky and hard to tune, considering many hyperparameters. Are the m and n in Eq. 6 the same for different datasets?

3. It’s hard to decide how the method works without a supplementary video, I doubt the view-consistency of the generated video across frames and views.

Questions:
1. How are the hyperparameters of the “progressive foreground enhancement” determined? Is it calculated using the distribution of different categories?

2. Will the appearance of the same objects change across different frames and views? Because the MPI encoder only provides semantic layout, not texture features, and there is no strict geometry constraint.

Rating:
4
